coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and curcumin-loaded CD44-Targeted liposomes for imaging and tracking cancer chemotherapy: A multi-purpose tool for theranostics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Demir, Bilal
Moulahoum, Hichem
Ghorbanizamani, Faezeh
Barlas, F. Baris
Yesiltepe, Ozan
Gumus, Z. Pinar
Meral, Kadem
Odaci Demirkol, Dilek
Timur, Suna
coredata.dc:description: Growing needs in biomedical applications have driven the development of multifunctional nanoparticles towards theranostically-engineered multimodal platforms. Many strategies for point-effective cellular uptake of theranostics have been described. In this report, multifunctional nanostructures were designed and synthesized as a novel theranostic platform. Liposomes were loaded with curcumin as a therapeutic agent and carbon dots (CDs) as a contrast molecule. Anti-CD44 antibodies were bioconjugated on the liposomal surface to obtain an active targeting nanocarrier. After synthesis, the relative characterization of the vesicles was performed using DLS and TEM techniques. In vitro analysis was conducted to investigate the multimodal properties of the theranostic via testing its toxicity, cell uptake, bioimaging, and chemo/radiotherapy applications on two different cell lines. 3D holographic microscopy was employed to track the treatment effects during experimentation. Results indicated that these particles are efficient tools for clinical applications due to their fluorescent characteristic and increased bioperformance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Theranostics
Carbon dots
Curcumin
Liposome
CD44
Bioconjugation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of liposomes with anti-CD44 antibody
Cell bioimaging using fluorescence and 3D holographic microscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the prepared theranostic CUR/CD/Anti-CD44 liposomes.

Visual and spectroscopy/fluorimetry analysis of the synthesized liposomes. (A) Photographs of the different liposome preparations observed by naked-eye and under UV-lamp. (B) UV/Visible spectroscopic spectrum. (C) Emission spectra of CD-containing liposomes (λex = 360 nm).

In vitro assays for the different liposomal formulations. (A and B) Effect of free CDs and Free CUR treatment on U-87MG and HaCaT cell lines. (C and D) Effect of the different liposome formulations on the viability of the U-87MG and HaCaT cell lines. F1: curcumin liposomes. F2: CUR/CD liposomes. F3: CUR/CD/anti-CD44 liposomes. (E) Comparison between the effect of 32 μg/mL curcumin theranostic liposomes (F3) on the viability of U-87MG and HaCaT cell lines. (F) Radiotherapy effect of the various liposomes under 5 Gy irradiation. Results are presented as mean ± SD (n = 4). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001 compared to the respective control.

Fluorescence microscopy observation of U-87MG and HaCaT cell lines treated with free CDs and CDs-containing liposomes (A). Analytical measurements of fluorescence (B) by determination of corrected total cell fluorescence (CTCF) (left panel) and average fluorescence/cell (right panel). Data is presented as mean ± SD (n = 6). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001 vs. CD. ##p < 0.01 and ###p < 0.001 vs. F2.

3D holographic microscopy results obtained over a 24 h period. Digital holographic images of U87-MG under the different treatments (A). Various parameters measured through the software including Cell viability (B), Average cell area (C), and Average cell thickness (D).
Composition of the different types of liposomes prepared for theranostic applications.
Characterization of the different liposome formulations using DLS.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and curcumin-loaded CD44-Targeted liposomes for imaging and tracking cancer chemotherapy: A multi-purpose tool for theranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Ege University, Bornova, Izmir, 35100, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Ege University, Bornova, Izmir, 35100, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Research Testing and Analysis Laboratory Research and Application Center, Ege University, Bornova, Izmir, 35100, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central Research Testing and Analysis Laboratory Research and Application Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Central Research Testing and Analysis Laboratory Research and Application Center, Ege University, Bornova, Izmir, 35100, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Private Practitioner of Chemistry, Erzurum, 25100, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Private Practitioner of Chemistry, Erzurum, 25100, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Ege University, Bornova, Izmir, 35100, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Growing needs in biomedical applications have driven the development of multifunctional nanoparticles towards theranostically-engineered multimodal platforms. Many strategies for point-effective cellular uptake of theranostics have been described. In this report, multifunctional nanostructures were designed and synthesized as a novel theranostic platform. Liposomes were loaded with curcumin as a therapeutic agent and carbon dots (CDs) as a contrast molecule. Anti-CD44 antibodies were bioconjugated on the liposomal surface to obtain an active targeting nanocarrier. After synthesis, the relative characterization of the vesicles was performed using DLS and TEM techniques. In vitro analysis was conducted to investigate the multimodal properties of the theranostic via testing its toxicity, cell uptake, bioimaging, and chemo/radiotherapy applications on two different cell lines. 3D holographic microscopy was employed to track the treatment effects during experimentation. Results indicated that these particles are efficient tools for clinical applications due to their fluorescent characteristic and increased bioperformance.

ce:section.ce:para: The application of nanomaterials in the field of biomedicine has seen a tremendous increase in recent years owing to their potential for drug delivery, therapy, and diagnosis especially in the subject of cancer treatment [1]. Generally, nano-scaled molecules are thought to passively gather inside the tumors due to their superior permeability features [2]. Nevertheless, the presence of multilayered barriers in the system tends to limit the general effectiveness of such small molecules in the system. Intravenously injected nanomolecules are prone to keep circulating for elongated durations if they are not sufficiently small to be eliminated by the kidney or adequately large to be quickly trapped by the reticuloendothelial system [3]. As such, nanoparticles (NPs) are confronted with a contradictory problem when delivering their contents: Large NPs exhibit weak tumor permeation while the small NPs are poorly retained inside the tumors [4]. Hence, it is important to overcome these issues in the process of developing new NPs.

The biomedical field has been in constant advance in the development of novel multifunctional nanosystems that are capable of imaging, diagnosis, and targeted drug delivery simultaneously [5,6]. There are two different approaches when addressing fluorescent imaging: one focuses on the use of fluorescent chemicals such as rhodamine while the other involves the use of synthetically fluorescent tags, NPs, or probes [7,8]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a carbon-based nanoparticle class possessing strong fluorescence properties [9]. They have become the center of interest given all the characteristics they demonstrate. CDs possess excellent optical and surface features that emphasize their potential in different applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and biosensing. They are more biocompatible, less toxic, photostable, and adaptable compared to the traditional quantum dots (QDs) [9]. It has been suggested that the fluorescence of CDs comes from both the core and surface which implies the possibility to adjust the fluorescence by modifying the functional groups on the surface [10,11].

Curcumin is a well-known nutraceutical possessing various benefits for human health. It has a multitude of established activities such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-cancer effects that makes it a good pharmacologic molecule [12]. However, the great potential of curcumin is greatly limited by its low solubility in water, degradation liability, and poor bioavailability [13]. In order to bypass these issues, many attempts were made to encapsulate the molecule in different cargo systems such as NPs and hallocytes nanotubes [14,15], nanocapsules [16], lipidic nanoemulsions such as liposomes [17], and hybrid polymeric vesicles such as hyalurosomes [18,19].

Modern advances in targeted nanoparticle-based therapy opened the door for interesting new possibilities in liposome-based systems [20,21]. This is more evident since liposomes are known to effectively target cells and facilitate the release of their content [22]. The screening for specific ligands with targeting potential is of great help to the current therapy strategies. CD44 is a transmembrane glycoprotein widely overexpressed in a variety of cancer cells and represents a great marker for targeted therapy. It has been shown that nanocarriers coated with anti-CD44 antibodies are able to successfully deliver their content to the targeted cell by specifically binding to the CD44 present on the cancer cell's surface [23].

Herein, we designed a multimodal liposomal platform for targeted therapeutic and bioimaging applications. The formulation was prepared with a double encapsulation of CDs in the aqueous region and curcumin in the lipid bilayer. Besides, an antibody anti-CD44 was attached to the surface for precise targeting of cancerous cells overexpressing the CD44 glycoprotein. The prepared platform is intended to enhance the delivery of multiple molecules for theranostic purposes which can avoid many of the hindrances faced in the circulation thanks to the specific targeting molecules on the surface.

Curcumin, MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide), DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide), EDC (N-(3- dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride), DTT (dithiothreitol), MES (4-Morfolinetansülfonik acid), SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate), Dimyristoylphosphatidylcholine (DMPC), Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody, methanol, and chloroform were procured from Sigma Aldrich (Dorset, UK). Cholesterol (99%) was obtained from Avanti Polar Lipids (Alabaster, Alabama, USA). Cell culture materials including Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), l-Glutamine (200 mM), Trypsin/EDTA (0.05% Trypsin; 0.20 g/L EDTA), Penicillin/Streptomycin (10,000 UI/mL), and Phosphate PBS were acquired from Lonza (Basel, Switzerland). FBS (Fetal Bovine Serum) was obtained from Biowest (Nuaillé, France). The rest of the chemicals used during the study are all of high analytical grade and were, unless described otherwise, obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using a liquid nitrogen-assisted synthesis method [24]. This method was modified by adding a solvothermal treatment procedure. After the liquid nitrogen-assisted synthesis process, the yielded solution of Blueberry was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 133 °C for 12 h. Then, the final solution was cooled to room temperature. The suspension was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 40 min and passed on a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The determination of the size, PDI, and zeta-potential of the CDs was done using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Malvern NanoZS, USA) while the shape of the particle was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The spectroscopic characterization of the obtained CDs was made using a UV/Vis spectrometric method. 200 μL of different CD concentrations (25–1000 μg/mL) were deposited in a 96-well microplate and the spectrum absorbance was acquired between 300 and 600 nm (Epoch Microplate Spectrophotometer, Biotek). On the other hand, the fluorescence analysis of CDs was performed by preparing a 250 μg/mL CD solution in a 3.0 mL distilled water and the fluorescence spectra was measured using a Varioskan Spectro fluorometer (Thermo Fischer). First, the emission spectrum (300–700 nm) was analyzed with different excitation wavelengths (300–480 nm with 20 nm intervals). Then, different CD concentrations (25–1000 μg/mL) were analyzed for the emission spectrum (350–700 nm) with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Liposomes were prepared using the traditional film hydration method as described in our previous study [25]. The components and concentrations for the preparation of the liposomes are detailed in Table 1 where they were mixed in a solution of Chloroform:Methanol (3:1 v/v). To this, curcumin solution (0.5 mg/mL) was supplemented to the lipid mixture and evaporated using a rotary evaporator (45 °C) until a thin lipid film is observed. CDs (final concentration of 300 μg/mL) were dissolved in PBS (pH7.4, 40 °C) and were used for the hydration process. After vortexing for 2 h, the samples were put in a Hamilton syringe for the extrusion process. The samples were passed through a polycarbonate membrane (80 nm pore size) 11 times followed by dialysis (MW: 6000–8000 kDa) overnight at +4 °C. The prepared liposomes were kept at +4 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of liposomes with anti-CD44 antibody
ce:section.ce:para: The next step consisted of preparing CUR/CD/Anti-CD44 liposomes using the post-modification method with EDC/NHS coupling reaction. In an Eppendorf, 48 mg EDC, 7.2 mg NHS, and 7.2 μg/mL anti-CD44 antibody were dissolved in 500 μL MES buffer (pH 6.0) and agitated (1000 rpm) at room temperature for 15 min. CUR/CD liposomes (1.5 mL) was added to the tube and incubated for 3 h under agitation (1000 rpm). The reaction activates the –NH2 and –COOH groups necessary to conjugate the liposomes with the antibody. The excess of NHS/EDC was eliminated via a 6 h dialysis at room temperature. The prepared bioconjugated liposomes are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The determination of the size, PDI, and zeta-potential of the liposomes was performed the same way as for CDs using the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique (Malvern NanoZS, USA). The different liposome formulations were analyzed through UV–Vis spectroscopy and for fluorescence (Varioskan Spectrofluorimeter (Thermo Fischer)).

Liposome samples (100 μL) were mixed with 900 μL of methanol, vortexed, and then sonicated for 10 min to break the lipid vesicles. The liberated curcumin was analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Agilent) with DAD detector (Santa Clara, CA, USA). 20 μL of the samples were injected at room temperature into the system and eluted through an Eclipse XDB-C18 column (5.0 μm particle size, 4.6 × 150 mm) using a mobile phase consisting of a mixture of (A) 5.0% (v/v) acetic acid and (B) acetonitrile (25:75, v/v). The flow rate was adjusted to 1.0 mL/min and the detector wavelength was set at 425 nm. A stock solution of curcumin (0.1–25 mg/L) was prepared in methanol and curcumin encapsulation was determined via the obtained calibration curve from HPLC with the equation of y = 179.97x + 22.385 (R2 = 0.999). The encapsulation efficacy of the CDs was similarly determined via fluorometric analysis and a standard curve prepared between 25 and 1000 μg/mL. Data was calculated using the equation y = 0.001x+0.1397 (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. S1).

The prepared CDs, curcumin, and the various liposome formulations were tested in cell culture to analyze both the chemo- and radiotherapeutic potential of these nanomaterials. HaCaT and U-87MG (ATCC, USA) cell lines were chosen to first test the toxicity of free CDs (0–150 μg/mL) by MTT test. From this, the highest non-toxic dose of CDs was chosen for the preparation of the liposomes and radiosensitivity tests. The presence of CDs in the vesicles allowed their bioimaging using their fluorescence properties under fluorescent microscopy (red filter). Concomitantly, curcumin liposomes were tested for their toxicity with different doses (0–160 μg/mL) for the IC50 determination.

The cell culture conditions applied in the current work were as follows: HaCaT and U-87MG cell lines were maintained in a DMEM culture medium (with l-Glutamine), 10% FBS, and 1.0% penicillin/streptomycin. The cells were incubated in a humidified atmosphere with 37 °C and 5.0% CO2. During experimental protocols, the cells were seeded at 4 × 103 cells/well in 4 replicates and left for adjustment (24 h). The next day, cells are treated with the different molecules in fresh media and incubated for another 24 h before proceeding for the viability test (MTT).

The effect of the different treatments on the cell line's viability was analyzed using the MTT test. After treatment and incubation of 24h, the cells were washed with PBS, and then, 110 μL of 10% MTT solution was added to each well. The plate was incubated for 4h to allow the formazan formation. Next, 100 μL of SDS (10% in 0.01 M HCl) was supplemented to stop the reaction and dissolve the formazan. Optical densities were obtained by a microplate reader at 570 and 630 nm. The results were calculated compared to the untreated control and expressed as percentage of viable cells.

The radiosensitivity of the liposomes (containing CDs) was assessed via irradiation using a linear accelerator (6 mV) according to a standard protocol [26]. Briefly, a 96-well plate containing 4 × 103 cells was incubated overnight after which the culture medium was eliminated, and the cells were irradiated with a force of 5.0 Gy using a linear accelerator system (LINAC; Siemens Primus, Kemnath, Germany). A negative control (cells with neither liposome nor irradiation treatment) and positive control (cells without liposomes but irradiated) were made. Cells were incubated for 72 h and an MTT test was performed according to the protocol described earlier.

Cells (U-87MG) at 8 × 103 cells were deposited in an 8-chamber slide and incubated for 48h. The culture medium was replaced and the slide was incubated for another 2 h before being washed 3 times with PBS. Nucleus staining was performed using a solution of DAPI and incubated for 15 min followed by 3 PBS washes and then put under a fluorescent microscope for observation (using the red filter). Results were compared to control groups. Analytical measurement was performed by analyzing different pictures taken from different fields (n = 6) of each group. The fluorescence was estimated by ImageJ software and data are presented as corrected total cell fluorescence (CTCF) according to the formula: CTCF = Integrated Density – (Area of selected cell × Mean fluorescence of background readings).

For the 3D holographic evaluation of liposome formulations in μ-channels, 3 × 104 cells were seeded on each slide (ibidi μ-Slide VI 0.4, ibidi GmbH, Germany) and allowed to adhere for 2 h. Each reservoir was filled with 60 μL cell-free medium as suggested by the manufacturer and was incubated for 22 h. 25 images were collected from each channel each hour and analyzed by Hstudio™ M3 ver 2.6.1 Tracking Software. An illustration of the set-up for the holographic system and images of the cells are shown in Fig. S2.

Results are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The statistical study was performed using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test (GraphPad Prism 5.0, California). The statistical differences were considered significant when p < 0.05.

The synthesized CDs were analyzed for their physicochemical and spectroscopic properties. During the process of CD synthesis from natural sources, their surface structure was modified via hydrothermal techniques. This approach was employed to make a higher hydrophilic character by using a long-time sonication on the raw ethanolic CD solution followed by evaporation. The particles were observed under TEM and the absorbance spectra of various concentrations of CDs were analyzed between 300 and 600 nm and a peak at 335 nm was observed (Figs. S3A–B). It has been suggested that the appearance of the characteristic peak of synthesized CDs is due to the presence of CO groups in their structures [24]. CDs with peaks between 227 and 299 nm are thought to come from the transitions of bonds in the carbon cores while higher peaks (eg. 440 nm) are assumed to be a result of a transition in the surface groups. These transitions are important for fluorescence emission determination [27].

In the aim to determine the fluorescent features of the synthesized CDs, a fluorescence spectra analysis was performed. The determination of the excitation wavelength of the CDs was obtained by analyzing a single dose of CDs (250 μg/mL) under different excitation wavelengths (20 nm intervals). The results obtained showed that excitation wavelengths between 300 and 500 nm induced a redshift of the emission peaks from 390 nm to 550 nm (Fig. S3C). Afterward, the excitation wavelength determination of the current CDs was performed using increasing concentrations with an emission wavelength of 360 nm determined from the earlier analysis (Fig. S3D). Most CDs demonstrate an excitation-dependent emission which suggests that the adjustability of CDs fluorescence could be attributed to the presence of functional groups on the surface of the molecules and the various bandgaps states [27–29].

CDs are known to be similar to quantum dots in their spectral and dimensional features [9]. In here, CDs have a hydrodynamic diameter of less than 10 nm. This is an important parameter in terms of its easy penetration into cells. Considering the physicochemical parameters of liposome formulations, it was found that the particle sizes were around 75 nm after extrusion. In addition, the decrease in surface load translated by the zeta-potential values after each modification added to the structure suggests that the theranostic liposomes are successfully synthesized. In lipid-based drug delivery systems, PDI is an important factor to describe the homogeneity of the vesicle's size distribution. Depending on the application intended, the accepted PDI value can change. In biomedicine applications. A PDI value of 0.3 is considered optimal yet until now, there are no suggested criteria for liposomes' PDI values by the FDA [33]. The encapsulation of curcumin was highly substantial reaching 91.2%, 88%, and 86% for the formulations F1 to F3, respectively. CD encapsulation in F2 and F3 reached 58.3% and 54.3%, respectively (Table 2).

The first step in cellular testing concerned the use of free CDs and explore their toxicity over two cell lines (U-87MG and HaCaT). The results of the viability test showed that there was low to no toxicity induced by CDs in the U-87MG cell line while HaCaT cells were more sensitive to CDs, showing signs of toxicity starting 75 μg/mL (Fig. 3 A). Similarly, free curcumin was tested over the two cell lines to determine the anti-cancer effect of the molecule and use it as a reference to demonstrate the enhancements seen through the encapsulation and targeting effect of the synthesized formulations (Fig. 3B). U-87MG cells did not show any signs of toxicity at low concentrations and reached a significant effect at a dose of 160 μg/mL (p < 0.01). HaCaT cells were more sensitive to curcumin treatment and showed a significant reduction in cell viability starting from 3.2 μg/mL.

The cytotoxic effects of the liposomal formulations prepared herein were tested on U-87MG and HaCaT are presented in Fig. 3C and D. F1 liposomes that encapsulate curcumin only showed a slight increase in the anticancer effect compared with free curcumin. Interestingly, the F2 formulation (CUR/CD liposome) demonstrated a lower effect compared with the other liposome formulations. It has been observed that the F3 formulation designed with a theranostic feature completely ablated the metabolic activity of the cells in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 3C and D). In addition, the IC50 value of the F3 formulation was found to be 0.4 μM curcumin (15.6 μg/mL). The concentrations of the liposomes were defined according to the curcumin concentration loaded inside. In Fig. 3E, a comparison of HaCaT and U-87MG cells treated with 32 μg/mL curcumin concentration (F3) showed that curcumin liposomes were more effective in U-87MGs. Curcumin has been described to possess a large panel of activities including anti-proliferative and pro-apoptotic effects in several human cancers. These effects are induced through molecular interactions and cellular signal pathways modulation [34–37]. In addition to the antioxidant effects seen with curcumin, the latter has been described to possess pro-oxidant characteristics at concentrations higher than 50 μM [38]. As such, the results obtained in the current work align with the information already known about the effects of curcumin in cancer therapy.

Targeting delivery platforms based on the use of antibodies represents a novel powerful tool for therapy. It can significantly boost the efficiency of the treatment while substantially reduce the toxicity effects observed. Additionally, the encapsulation of therapeutic drugs and contrast agents for a multimodal platform would supply another improvement for cancer theranostics research [23].

Although research on CDs is limited and is still in constant development, there is an increasing belief that carbon-based structures have a radiotherapeutic activity. Hence, we explored the effects of the prepared liposomes on cancer cells under radiation treatment. The U-87MG cells were treated with liposomes and put under 5.0 Gy radiation. The results showed that cell viability of the control group (no liposome treatment but irradiated) and F1 group showed similar results with 60% of the cells left alive. In the presence of CDs, the addition of radiation to the liposome treatments induced an increase in efficacy with 40% and 30% viability for F2 and F3, respectively (Fig. 3F). The presence of the anti-CD44 antibody over the liposome's surface significantly enhanced the therapeutic effects compared to all other groups (p < 0.001) which further emphasizes the potential of this multimodal theranostic formulation. In addition to the effect of curcumin as a stand-alone treatment molecule, various studies reported its potential as a complement to conventional chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Its use is thought to overcome radioresistance and increase their susceptibility to radiation treatments [35,37,39]. The radiosensitization actions of curcumin are possibly regulated through various mechanisms yet, the mechanism of action of curcumin with radiation treatment is still not fully understood [40]. There are not many reports on the effects of CDs as radiotherapy agents, however, some earlier reports addressed the therapeutic effect of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) after tumor cell irradiation [41,42]. Another report investigated the combination of CDs with gadolinium (Gd) and showed their radiotherapeutic effect [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell bioimaging using fluorescence and 3D holographic microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake profile of CD-containing structures and the free CDs were evaluated in the two cell lines using the intrinsic fluorescence properties of CDs. As expected, the targeted liposomal formulation (F3) showed the best cell uptake within U-87MG cells (Fig. 4 A). The corrected total cell fluorescence (CTCF) in cells treated with CDs alone showed a high level of fluorescence which can be attributed to the small size of the particles. F2 had lower CTCF levels in both cell lines that can be attributed to the encapsulation of CDs in the liposome and their controlled release. Lastly, F3 demonstrated the highest CTCF level thanks to the targeting antibodies present on the surface which enhances the delivery into the cells (Fig. 4B). The average fluorescence by cell was also measured and demonstrated the same pattern as the CTCF (Fig. 4B).

Given the data obtained, the drug delivery potential demonstrated by the encapsulation is demonstrated for different cell types (Fig. 3A–D and Fig.4). Drug delivery to the brain is an important aspect of the development of nano-encapsulation methods due to the presence of the blood-brain barrier in which, many molecules are unable to cross the barrier and reach their targets. Therefore, the following experiments focused on U-87MG cells as a representative example to avoid redundancy.

With the development of cell analysis systems, more effective research and test opportunities with different approaches and fewer substances are becoming more fashionable. Digital holographic microscopy has been demonstrated as an effective equipment in the case of label-free monitoring and tracking of cell populations by extracting many parameters under in vitro conditions [44]. This system can also be used for the visualization of some cellular processes such as cell death [45], cell cycle [46], cellular uptake [47], and cell morphology changes [48]. 3D holographic microscopy systems are one of these interesting approaches and are even seen as close to flow cytometry. The last stage of this study explored the theranostic application of the prepared formulations (Fig. 5 ). F2 group was not explored for this step (Fig. 5A) due to the cell viability data observed earlier where it did not demonstrate sufficient therapeutic effects compared with F1 and F3 (Fig. 3C and D). After the cells were cultivated and treated with liposomes in μ-slides, they were observed under a microscope for 24h. Results showed that the theranostic liposomes (F3) were able to show their potential compared to curcumin loaded liposomes (F1) starting from the sixteenth hour (Fig. 5B).

Curcumin loaded vesicles (8.0 μg/mL) were used to visualize the cellular morphology and the possible apoptosis effect under μ-channel slides. Fig. 5A shows the comparison of cellular morphology and relative behavior in the presence of the different treatments for 24 h. Control cells illustrated their typical morphological characteristics by attaching to the surface with well-formed networks. It is known that cell morphology undertakes an important alteration in the presence of treatment molecules. F3 formulation clearly shows a difference in the general U87-MG morphology. Furthermore, a higher cell thickness and smaller cell surface areas are observed, which could indicate a sign of apoptosis. Bright-field images of the same samples (Fig. S4) and average cell thickness and area (Fig. 5C and D) results are extracted by analyzing these images taken by digital holographic microscopy. F3 formulation has a considerable impact on cellular morphology as a potential therapeutic agent. The morphological changes of the cell surface and membrane thickness have been closely linked with cell status. Increased thickness and shrinkage of the cell area are markers of cell death mechanisms [49,50].

Antibodies play a pivotal role in cancer therapy via their use as targeting agents attached to nano-sized delivery systems. Antibody-targeted nanoparticles are highly specific to their targets which lower significantly the side effects and improve their half-life in the circulation [51]. Furthermore, the choice of the delivery system/nanoparticles, antibody, and drug types are of utmost importance because the development of highly performant systems is complex and requires taking account of many factors such as the interaction with cells and their uptake. The advances seen in the therapeutic field have shifted the focus from the use of whole antibodies and started implementing strategies toward “minimization” by developing antibody fragments-NPs conjugates which could be seen as an important theranostic tool [52].

A novel multimodal platform using CDs, curcumin, and anti-CD44 antibody combination over liposomes were formulated for theranostics purposes. Based on the results obtained during this study, the prepared theranostic liposomes (containing the targeting anti-CD44 antibody) showed an enhanced effect on cancer cells compared to curcumin-loaded and CUR/CD-loaded liposomes. Besides, the radiosensitivity and bioimaging potential of CDs addition emphasized the potential of such a combination as a good tool for real-time diagnostic. All these evidences suggest that our proposed theranostic platform would be a promising strategy towards the design of highly smart nanoformulations with enhanced effectivity in cancer theranostics.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Bilal Demir: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Hichem Moulahoum: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Faezeh Ghorbanizamani: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. F. Baris Barlas: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ozan Yesiltepe: Investigation. Z. Pinar Gumus: Investigation. Kadem Meral: Investigation. Dilek Odaci Demirkol: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Supervision. Suna Timur: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ege University, Scientific Research Foundation is acknowledged for the support (Project no: 2017 FEN 007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2021.102363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on intracellular delivery of liposome encapsulated quantum dots using advanced fluorescence microscopy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A retrospective 30 Years after discovery of the enhanced permeability and retention effect of solid tumors: next-generation chemotherapeutics and photodynamic therapy--problems, solutions, and prospects
